# fastactor

> Elixir/OTP-style actors for Python, built on [anyio](https://anyio.readthedocs.io/). Processes with mailboxes, GenServers with `call`/`cast`, supervisors with `one_for_one` / `one_for_all` / `rest_for_one` restart strategies, `DynamicSupervisor`, `Registry` (unique / duplicate), `Agent`, awaitable `Task`, links, monitors, `trap_exits`, `handle_continue`, and named registration — all faithful to the OTP semantics you know from Elixir, translated to idiomatic Python with `async` / `await` and `anyio` task groups.

This file is the canonical reference for agents writing code against `fastactor`. Every public class, method, message type, and exception is documented with exact signatures and behavior. If you need a concept-level tour, read the "Mental model" section first; if you know OTP and just need the Python API, jump straight to "API reference".

---

## Orientation

- **Import root:** `from fastactor.otp import ...` re-exports the entire public surface.
- **Runtime requirement:** every actor operation requires an active `Runtime` context (`async with Runtime(): ...`). Exactly one Runtime may be active per process.
- **Concurrency model:** `anyio` structured concurrency. Every actor runs inside the Runtime's `TaskGroup`; cancellation and teardown are propagated structurally.
- **Python:** 3.13+.
- **Mailbox:** bounded anyio memory object stream, capacity from `settings.mailbox_size` (default 1024, env var `FASTACTOR_MAILBOX_SIZE`).

---

## Mental model — Elixir ↔ Python

Both columns show the **call you actually write** — your subclass / module on the user-code rows, the framework name on the framework rows.

| Elixir / OTP                                            | fastactor                                                 | Notes                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `MyWorker.start_link(arg)`                              | `await MyWorker.start_link(arg)`                          | Always `await`. `start_link` is inherited — you call it on your subclass.                    |
| `GenServer.call(pid, :req)`                             | `await worker.call(request, timeout=5)`                   | Raises on timeout or on callback exception.                                                  |
| `GenServer.cast(pid, :req)`                             | `worker.cast(request)`                                    | Fire-and-forget, synchronous call (not awaitable).                                           |
| `handle_call({:foo, bar}, _from, state)`                | `handle_call(self, call: Call)` — match `call.message`    | Bare return value becomes the reply; return `None` to defer (see below).                     |
| `{:noreply, state}` / `GenServer.reply(from, v)`        | `return None`, then later `call.set_result(v)`            | No sentinel class — `None` return means "don't reply yet"; caller stashes `call` and replies later. |
| `{:stop, reason, reply, state}`                         | `return (reply, Stop(reason))`                            | Caller gets `reply`, then the server stops with `reason`. Symmetric with `(reply, Continue(term))`. |
| `{:noreply, state, {:continue, arg}}`                   | `return Continue("arg")`                                  | `handle_continue` runs next, before the next mailbox read.                                   |
| `{:via, Registry, {R, key}}`                            | `await Worker.start(via=("R", key))`                      | Mutually exclusive with `name=`. Also returns by `whereis(("R", key))`.                      |
| `Process.link(pid)` / `Process.monitor(pid)`            | `proc.link(other)` / `proc.monitor(other)`                | Bidirectional link; unidirectional monitor emits `Down` when the target terminates.          |
| `Process.flag(:trap_exit, true)`                        | `await MyWorker.start_link(trap_exits=True)`              | Convert link cascades to `handle_exit(message: Exit)` callbacks.                             |
| `Supervisor.start_link(..., strategy: :one_for_one)`    | `await Supervisor.start(strategy="one_for_one")`          | Plus `one_for_all` and `rest_for_one`.                                                       |
| `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(sup, spec)`              | `await dsup.start_child(spec)`                            | Always `one_for_one`; supports `max_children` and `extra_arguments`.                         |
| `Registry.start_link(keys: :unique, name: R)`           | `await Registry.new("R", mode="unique")`                  | Two modes: `unique` (1 pid per key) and `duplicate` (pub/sub fan-out).                       |
| `Agent.start_link(fn -> 0 end)`                         | `await Agent.start(lambda: 0)`                            | Tiny state-holder over `GenServer`.                                                          |
| `Task.async(fn)` / `Task.async_nolink(fn)`              | `await Task.start_link(fn)` / `await Task.start(fn)`      | `start_link` cascades a crash to caller; `start` does not. Consume with `result = await t`.  |

The names match where the Python idiom allows; divergences are intentional and annotated here so you don't guess.

---

## Installation

Requires Python 3.13+. This repo uses [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) and [mise](https://mise.jdx.dev/):

```bash
uv sync
uv run pytest src
```

There is no PyPI release yet — install from source:

```bash
uv add "fastactor @ git+https://github.com/CyrusNuevoDia/fastactor"
```

---

## The Runtime

Everything runs inside an active `Runtime`. It owns the anyio task group, the top-level `RuntimeSupervisor`, and the registries.

```python
from fastactor.otp import Runtime

async def main():
    async with Runtime() as rt:
        # all actor work happens here
        ...
```

Inside any actor (or coroutine running inside the runtime), reach it with `Runtime.current()`. Only one Runtime may be active at a time — a second `async with Runtime():` raises `RuntimeError("Runtime already started")`.

### Runtime API

```python
class Runtime:
    supervisor: RuntimeSupervisor | None       # top-level supervisor with trap_exits=True
    registry: dict[str, str]                   # name → proc.id (named-process lookup)
    processes: dict[str, Process]              # proc.id → Process
    registries: dict[str, _RegistryEntry]      # user-facing Registry storage

    @classmethod
    def current(cls) -> "Runtime": ...         # raises RuntimeError if none active

    async def spawn[P: Process](
        self, process: P, *args, name: str | None = None, **kwargs
    ) -> P: ...

    def register_name(self, name: str, proc: Process): ...
    def unregister_name(self, name: str): ...
    def where_is(self, name: str) -> Process | None: ...                     # sync, str-only shim
    async def whereis(
        self, name_or_via: str | tuple[str, Any]
    ) -> Process | None: ...                                                  # async, accepts name or (registry, key)
```

`spawn` accepts either `name=` (global name) or `via=(registry, key)` (registers into a `Registry` entry), not both. You rarely call `spawn` directly — `Process.start` / `Process.start_link` do it for you. `register_name` / `where_is` back the legacy named-registration feature; prefer `whereis(...)` in new code — it's the canonical async lookup and handles both string names and `(registry, key)` tuples.

There's also a package-level free function:

```python
from fastactor.otp import whereis

proc = await whereis("my_singleton")
proc = await whereis(("sessions", user_id))
```

### Entry points

Three equivalent ways to bring up a Runtime. All install `SIGINT` / `SIGTERM` traps by default (opt out via `trap_signals=False`); receiving a signal cancels the runtime's task group and triggers `__aexit__` cleanup inside the live loop.

```python
# 1. Context manager — primary. Best for tests, library code, anywhere inside an existing async scope.
async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        counter = await Counter.start()
        ...
anyio.run(main)

# 2. fastactor.run(main) — one-line app entry point. Wraps anyio.run + Runtime + clean-Ctrl-C handling.
import fastactor
fastactor.run(main)                     # returns main's return value
fastactor.run(main, trap_signals=False) # opt out of signal handling

# 3. Runtime.start() / Runtime.stop() — explicit pair. Best for REPLs/Jupyter where `async with` is awkward.
rt = await Runtime.start()              # enters the runtime; returns it
counter = await Counter.start()
await rt.stop()                         # equivalent to leaving `async with`
```

`Runtime.start()` enforces the singleton the same way the context manager does — a second `start()` raises `RuntimeError("Runtime already started")`. `Runtime.stop()` is idempotent after the first call clears the singleton.

### `fastactor.run()` signature

```python
def run(
    main_fn: Callable[[], Awaitable[Any]],
    *,
    trap_signals: bool = True,
    backend: str = "asyncio",
) -> Any
```

When `trap_signals=True` (default), a signal-triggered `CancelledError` (bubbling up from `main_fn` because the runtime's task group got cancelled) is silently absorbed — `run()` returns `None`. Normal exceptions still propagate unchanged. `BaseExceptionGroup` with a single inner exception is automatically unwrapped inside `Runtime.__aexit__` so callers see the original error, not anyio's group wrapper.

### Why there is no "auto-cleanup on process exit"

Python's `atexit` hooks run synchronously after the event loop has already closed. There's no reliable way to schedule `await rt.__aexit__()` from a sync finalizer. Signal handlers give the same effective guarantee within the live loop: `SIGINT`/`SIGTERM` arrive → task group cancels → `__aexit__` cleans up → process exits. That's why `trap_signals=True` is the default.

---

## Process — the base actor

`Process` is the abstract base. It has a bounded mailbox, a `_started` / `_stopped` pair of events, `links` / `monitors` / `monitored_by` sets, and a lifecycle driven by `loop()`. Subclass it and override the callbacks you need.

### Callbacks (override these)

```python
async def init(self, *args, **kwargs) -> Ignore | Stop | Continue | None:
    """Runs once before the mailbox loop. Return value controls what happens next."""
    return None

async def handle_info(self, message: Any) -> Continue | None:
    """Called for every non-standard message (anything not Stop/Exit/Call/Cast)."""

async def handle_exit(self, message: Exit):
    """Only called when trap_exits=True and a linked process exits."""

async def handle_continue(self, term: Any):
    """Runs next after a callback returns Continue(term)."""

async def on_terminate(self, reason: Any):
    """User cleanup hook. Runs BEFORE monitors/links are notified, so observers see the
    post-cleanup state. Exceptions here are logged but do NOT block the Down/Exit fanout.
    Prefer this over overriding `terminate`."""

async def terminate(self, reason: Any):
    """Machinery teardown — calls `on_terminate`, notifies monitors/links, unregisters.
    You almost never override this; override `on_terminate` instead."""
```

### Starting, stopping, sending

```python
# Factory methods (classmethods)
await Worker.start(*args, trap_exits=False, supervisor=None, name=None, via=None, **kwargs)
await Worker.start_link(*args, trap_exits=False, supervisor=None, name=None, via=None, **kwargs)
# `name=` registers under the runtime's global name map; `via=(registry, key)` registers
# into a user-created Registry. They are mutually exclusive.

# Instance methods
await proc.send(msg)                # async send; waits if mailbox is full
proc.send_nowait(msg)               # raises if full
await proc.stop(reason="normal", timeout=60, sender=None)
await proc.kill()                   # closes the mailbox, unwinds immediately
proc.info(msg, sender=None)         # wraps in Info(sender, msg) and send_nowait

# Lifecycle observation
proc.has_started() -> bool
await proc.started() -> Self        # waits until init completes
proc.has_stopped() -> bool
await proc.stopped() -> Self        # waits until terminate completes
```

### Init return sentinels

`init` can return any of:

| Return                 | Effect                                                                        |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `None`                 | Normal start, enter mailbox loop.                                             |
| `Ignore()`             | Skip the loop, never run; `_started` and `_stopped` both set.                 |
| `Stop(reason=...)`     | Refuse to start; emits `Shutdown(reason)`; crashes if reason is abnormal.     |
| `Continue(term)`       | Start normally, but run `handle_continue(term)` before the first mailbox read. |

### Minimal Process example

```python
from fastactor.otp import Process, Runtime

class Worker(Process):
    async def init(self, *, name: str):
        self.name = name

    async def handle_info(self, message):
        print(self.name, "got", message)

async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        p = await Worker.start(name="alice")
        await p.send("hello")
        await p.stop("normal")
```

### Equality and identity

`Process.__hash__` and `__eq__` are by `id` (a ksuid string from `utils.id_generator`). Two `Process` instances with the same id compare equal — this is what makes `links`/`monitors` sets work.

---

## GenServer — synchronous call + async cast

`GenServer` inherits from `Process` and adds `call` (synchronous, awaits a reply) and `cast` (fire-and-forget).

### Callbacks

```python
async def handle_call(
    self, call: Call,
) -> Any | None | tuple[Any, Continue] | tuple[Any, Stop]:
    # Return contract — picked one of these:
    #   None                         → NoReply; caller stays blocked. Stash `call` and later
    #                                  invoke `call.set_result(value)` to resolve the reply.
    #   value                        → Reply with `value`.
    #   (value, Continue(term))      → Reply with `value`, then run handle_continue(term).
    #   (value, Stop(reason))        → Reply with `value`, then stop with `reason`.
    #   raise                        → Exception propagates to caller AND crashes the server.
    return call.message

async def handle_cast(self, cast: Cast) -> Continue | None:
    # no reply; optionally return Continue(term) to schedule handle_continue
    ...

async def handle_info(self, message) -> Continue | None: ...
```

### Client API

```python
await server.call(request, sender: Process | None = None, timeout=5) -> Any
server.cast(request, sender: Process | None = None)           # sync call, not awaitable
```

`sender` defaults to the current process (via `_current_process` ContextVar) or the supervisor; you rarely set it manually. `call` raises `TimeoutError` on timeout and re-raises any exception thrown inside `handle_call`.

### Deferred reply — `handle_call` returning `None`

Returning `None` from `handle_call` means "don't reply yet". Stash the `Call` object, finish your callback, and later — from `handle_info`, `handle_continue`, or another `handle_call` — call `saved_call.set_result(value)` to unblock the waiting caller. This is the fastactor equivalent of Elixir's `{:noreply, state}` + `GenServer.reply(from, v)`.

```python
class Deferred(GenServer):
    async def init(self):
        self.pending: Call | None = None

    async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
        if call.message == "wait":
            self.pending = call
            return None                    # NoReply — caller stays blocked on `await server.call("wait")`
        if call.message == "release":
            if self.pending is not None:
                self.pending.set_result("done")   # resolves the earlier blocked call
                self.pending = None
            return "ok"
```

Because `None` is the sentinel, if you ever need to reply with the literal value `None`, use the tuple form `return (None, Continue("done"))` or resolve via `call.set_result(None)` explicitly.

### Call and Cast message wrappers

IMPORTANT: the callbacks receive wrapped messages. Unpack with `.message`:

```python
async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
    match call.message:
        case ("get", key):      return self.store[key]
        case ("put", key, val): self.store[key] = val; return "ok"
```

This wrapper convention is deliberate and stable. Do NOT write `async def handle_call(self, request, from_, state):` — that's Elixir, not fastactor.

### If handle_call raises

The exception is both delivered to the caller (re-raised from `await server.call(...)`) AND re-raised inside the server process, which then crashes. This mirrors Elixir's `GenServer` semantics — a buggy `handle_call` shouldn't just lie to the caller.

### Full Counter example

```python
from fastactor.otp import GenServer, Runtime, Call, Cast

class Counter(GenServer):
    count: int

    async def init(self, start: int = 0):
        self.count = start

    async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
        return self.count

    async def handle_cast(self, cast: Cast):
        match cast.message:
            case ("add", n): self.count += n
            case ("reset",): self.count = 0

async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        counter = await Counter.start(start=10)
        counter.cast(("add", 5))
        counter.cast(("add", 2))
        assert await counter.call(None) == 17       # mailbox is FIFO, casts have been processed
        await counter.stop()
```

Notice that a subsequent `call` round-trip guarantees prior casts have been processed. This is the **standard deterministic-sync pattern** in fastactor — use it instead of `asyncio.sleep` in tests.

---

## Links, monitors, trap_exits

Two process relationships, both OTP-identical:

- **Link** — bidirectional. If either side exits abnormally, the other is stopped with the same reason. If a linked process has `trap_exits=True`, it instead receives `Exit(sender, reason)` via `handle_exit`.
- **Monitor** — one-way. When the monitored process terminates (for any reason), the monitor receives `Down(sender, reason)` via `handle_info` and keeps running.

```python
from fastactor.otp import Down, GenServer, Runtime

class Watcher(GenServer):
    async def init(self):
        self.downs: list[Down] = []

    async def handle_info(self, msg):
        if isinstance(msg, Down):
            self.downs.append(msg)

async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        watcher = await Watcher.start()
        target  = await GenServer.start()
        watcher.monitor(target)

        await target.stop("normal")
        await target.stopped()              # Down now in watcher's mailbox
        # ... watcher.downs has one Down(target, "normal")
```

### What counts as a "normal" exit

Only these reasons are treated as normal (do not cascade across links, do not trigger `transient` restarts):

- `"normal"`
- `"shutdown"`
- an instance of `Shutdown(...)`

Everything else is a crash and propagates.

### Link / monitor operations

```python
proc.link(other)
proc.unlink(other)
proc.monitor(other)
proc.demonitor(other)
```

All are synchronous; they mutate the local sets on both processes.

---

## Supervisor — restart strategies and child specs

A `Supervisor` runs children according to a restart policy. Build child specs with `Supervisor.child_spec(...)` and start them with `start_child(spec)`.

### Strategies

| `strategy`     | On child exit                                                                                   |
| -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `one_for_one`  | Restart just the failing child.                                                                 |
| `one_for_all`  | Terminate all siblings (in reverse spec order) and restart them in forward order.               |
| `rest_for_one` | Restart the failing child plus every child started after it; earlier siblings keep running.    |

### Restart types (per child)

| `restart`   | Normal exit   | Crash        |
| ----------- | ------------- | ------------ |
| `permanent` | restart       | restart      |
| `transient` | don't restart | restart      |
| `temporary` | don't restart | don't restart |

### Shutdown types (per child)

| `shutdown`       | Behavior                                                                          |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `int` (seconds)  | `proc.stop(reason, timeout=shutdown)`; kill on timeout.                           |
| `"infinity"`     | `proc.stop(reason, timeout=None)`. Wait forever.                                  |
| `"brutal_kill"`  | `proc.kill()` immediately — closes the mailbox, no terminate callback completes.  |

### Restart intensity

`max_restarts` (default 3) / `max_seconds` (default 5.0) cap how often the supervisor will restart. Exceeding the cap crashes the supervisor itself with `Failed("Max restart intensity reached")`, which then propagates to its own supervisor.

### API

```python
# Construction
spec = Supervisor.child_spec(
    child_id: str,           # unique within this supervisor; required
    func_or_class,           # class with start_link (preferred) or async callable
    args: tuple | None = None,
    kwargs: dict | None = None,
    restart: RestartType = "permanent",
    shutdown: ShutdownType = 5,
    type: Literal["worker", "supervisor"] = "worker",
    modules: list | None = None,
    significant: bool = False,
) -> ChildSpec

sup = await Supervisor.start(
    strategy="one_for_one",  # or "one_for_all" / "rest_for_one"
    max_restarts=3,
    max_seconds=5,
    children=[spec_or_tuple, ...],   # optional; seeds before the strategy loop starts
)

# Management (mirrors Elixir)
proc   = await sup.start_child(spec_or_tuple)     # ChildSpec OR (cls, kwargs_dict) tuple
await sup.terminate_child(child_id)
sup.delete_child(child_id)                # spec must have been terminated first
proc   = await sup.restart_child(child_id) # for stopped children
infos  = sup.which_children()              # list of dicts with id/process/restart/shutdown/type/significant
counts = sup.count_children()              # {"specs": n, "active": n, "workers": n, "supervisors": n}
```

### Tuple-form child specs

Both `Supervisor` and `DynamicSupervisor` accept a 2-tuple `(cls, kwargs_dict)` anywhere a `ChildSpec` is expected — `start_child`, `children=` seeding, and the `_normalize_child_spec` helper. The child id defaults to `cls.__name__`; collisions are still rejected.

```python
# All three forms are equivalent for typical workers:
await sup.start_child(Supervisor.child_spec("counter", Counter, kwargs={"start": 0}))
await sup.start_child((Counter, {"start": 0}))                              # id = "Counter"
sup = await Supervisor.start(children=[(Counter, {"start": 0}), DbConnSpec])
```

### The root supervisor

`Runtime.supervisor` is a pre-installed `RuntimeSupervisor` (with `trap_exits=True`). When you call `Worker.start_link()` without an explicit `supervisor=`, the link is made to the root. For real applications, start your own `Supervisor` as a child of the root and hang your worker tree off that.

### Example

```python
from fastactor.otp import Runtime, Supervisor

async with Runtime() as rt:
    sup = rt.supervisor
    spec = sup.child_spec(
        "counter",
        Counter,
        kwargs={"start": 0},
        restart="transient",
        shutdown=5,
    )
    counter = await sup.start_child(spec)
```

---

## DynamicSupervisor — runtime-added children

Children aren't declared up front; they're added one-at-a-time. Strategy is always `one_for_one`.

### API

```python
pool = await DynamicSupervisor.start(
    max_children: int | float = float("inf"),
    extra_arguments: tuple = (),     # prepended to every child's start tuple
)

# Dynamic children default to restart="temporary"
spec = DynamicSupervisor.child_spec(
    None,                    # pass None to auto-generate a "dyn:..." id
    Worker,
    kwargs={"name": "alice"},
    restart="temporary",     # default
)
worker = await pool.start_child(spec)
```

Differences from `Supervisor`:

- `child_id` can be `None` → auto-generated `"dyn:<ksuid>"`.
- Default `restart` is `"temporary"` instead of `"permanent"`.
- Passing `strategy != "one_for_one"` in `init` raises `ValueError` — will surface as the original exception from `start()`.

### Example

```python
from fastactor.otp import DynamicSupervisor, Runtime

async with Runtime():
    pool = await DynamicSupervisor.start(max_children=10)
    for i in range(3):
        spec = pool.child_spec(None, Worker, kwargs={"name": f"w{i}"})
        await pool.start_child(spec)
```

---

## Registry — name-keyed process lookup

A `Registry` is a keyed map from arbitrary keys to processes. Distinct from `Runtime.register_name`, which is for a single *global* name per process.

Two modes:

- `unique` — one process per key. Re-registering raises `AlreadyRegistered(pid)`.
- `duplicate` — many processes per key. Useful for pub/sub fan-out.

Entries auto-scrub when a process terminates (hooked into `Runtime.unregister(proc)` via `_proc_keys`).

### API (all classmethods, all async)

```python
await Registry.new(name: str, mode: Literal["unique", "duplicate"]) -> None
await Registry.register(name, key, proc: Process) -> None        # raises AlreadyRegistered for unique collisions
await Registry.unregister(name, key, proc: Process) -> None
await Registry.lookup(name, key) -> list[Process]                # always a list; len <= 1 for unique
await Registry.dispatch(name, key, fn: Callable[[Process], Awaitable[None]]) -> None
await Registry.keys(name: str, proc: Process) -> list
```

`dispatch` snapshots the pid set under the registry lock, releases the lock, and then awaits `fn(proc)` for each. Exceptions in `fn` are logged and swallowed so one slow/broken subscriber can't break the fan-out.

### Example — pub/sub fan-out

```python
from fastactor.otp import Registry, Runtime

async with Runtime():
    await Registry.new("events", mode="duplicate")
    sub1 = await Subscriber.start(); await Registry.register("events", "news", sub1)
    sub2 = await Subscriber.start(); await Registry.register("events", "news", sub2)

    async def notify(proc):
        await proc.send(Info(None, "breaking news"))
    await Registry.dispatch("events", "news", notify)

    procs = await Registry.lookup("events", "news")  # [sub1, sub2] (order not stable)
```

---

## Agent — tiny state-holder

A thin wrapper over `GenServer` for "I just need to hold some state". Callbacks run inside the agent process; state is whatever the factory returns.

### API

```python
counter = await Agent.start(factory: Callable[[], S | Awaitable[S]])

await agent.get(fn: Callable[[S], R | Awaitable[R]], timeout=5) -> R
await agent.update(fn: Callable[[S], S | Awaitable[S]], timeout=5) -> None
await agent.get_and_update(fn: Callable[[S], tuple[R, S] | Awaitable[tuple[R, S]]], timeout=5) -> R
agent.cast_update(fn)                      # fire-and-forget state update
```

Functions may be sync or async — `_maybe_await` handles both.

### Example

```python
from fastactor.otp import Agent, Runtime

async with Runtime():
    counter = await Agent.start(lambda: 0)
    await counter.update(lambda n: n + 1)
    await counter.update(lambda n: n + 1)
    assert await counter.get(lambda n: n) == 2
    assert await counter.get_and_update(lambda n: (n, n * 10)) == 2
    assert await counter.get(lambda n: n) == 20
```

---

## Task — one-shot awaitable coroutines

A `Task` is a `Process` that runs a single coroutine once, captures the result (or exception), and exposes it through `__await__`. It has two flavors — exactly the equivalent of Elixir's `Task.async_nolink` vs `Task.async`:

| Factory                       | Link?   | Elixir analogue          | Failure behavior                                    |
| ----------------------------- | ------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------- |
| `Task.start(fn, ...)`         | No      | `Task.async_nolink`      | Caller survives a task crash; inspect via `await t`. |
| `Task.start_link(fn, ...)`    | Yes     | `Task.async`             | A task crash cascades to the caller via link.       |

### Consumption — `await task`

```python
task = await Task.start(compute)    # returns immediately — task runs in background
result = await task                 # blocks until done, raises if task raised
```

Timeouts are the caller's responsibility — wrap the await:

```python
from anyio import fail_after

with fail_after(5):
    result = await task
```

### Task post-start operations

```python
# Tri-state poll — does NOT raise on crash:
#   returns the result on success,
#   returns the Exception object on crash,
#   returns None on timeout.
result = await task.yield_(timeout=1)

# Graceful stop; falls back to kill() if the task doesn't stop within `timeout`.
await task.shutdown(timeout=5)
```

### TaskSupervisor — pool of tasks

```python
tsup = await TaskSupervisor.start()
task = await tsup.async_(compute, arg1, arg2, kwarg=value)   # primary name
task = await tsup.run(compute, arg1, arg2, kwarg=value)      # backward-compat alias
result = await task
```

`TaskSupervisor` is a `DynamicSupervisor` under the hood. `async_` is the idiomatic name (Elixir calls it `Task.Supervisor.async/2`); the trailing underscore is only because `async` is a Python keyword. `run` is preserved as an alias.

### Example — fan-out parallelism

```python
from fastactor.otp import Task, Runtime
from anyio import fail_after

async def fetch(url): ...

async with Runtime():
    tasks = [await Task.start(fetch, url) for url in urls]
    with fail_after(10):
        results = [await t for t in tasks]
```

---

## handle_continue — post-callback continuations

Elixir-style `{:continue, term}`. Schedule a follow-up callback without re-entering the mailbox loop. Useful for deferred init, cleanup after a call, or chaining long work.

### Mechanics

- `init` may return `Continue(term)` → `handle_continue(term)` runs before the first mailbox read.
- `handle_call` may return `(reply, Continue(term))` → caller gets `reply`, then `handle_continue(term)` runs before the next message.
- `handle_cast` / `handle_info` may return `Continue(term)` → `handle_continue(term)` runs next.

### Example — deferred init

```python
from fastactor.otp import GenServer, Continue

class Bootstrap(GenServer):
    async def init(self, db_url):
        self.db_url = db_url
        return Continue("load")             # init returns immediately; load happens next

    async def handle_continue(self, term):
        if term == "load":
            self.conn = await connect(self.db_url)
```

### Example — call with follow-up

```python
class Server(GenServer):
    async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
        reply = self._compute(call.message)
        return (reply, Continue(("log", reply)))   # reply delivered, then handle_continue

    async def handle_continue(self, term):
        match term:
            case ("log", reply):
                await self._log(reply)
```

---

## Named registration at start()

Two mutually-exclusive ways to make a process discoverable at construction time:

### `name=` — the global name map

```python
from fastactor.otp import whereis

g = await GenServer.start(name="sessions")
assert await whereis("sessions") is g

# A second start with the same live name raises:
#   Failed("already_started: sessions")
```

### `via=(registry_name, key)` — into a Registry

```python
from fastactor.otp import Registry, whereis

await Registry.new("sessions", mode="unique")
g = await GenServer.start(via=("sessions", user_id))
assert await whereis(("sessions", user_id)) is g

# Collisions in a unique registry raise AlreadyRegistered(pid) from the Registry layer.
```

Names and via-registrations both auto-free when the process terminates. Use `name=` for one-off singletons (one database client, one session manager). Use `via=` when you have many keyed instances (one session per user, one worker per tenant) — the `Registry` side also supports `duplicate` mode for pub/sub fan-out.

Specifying both `name=` and `via=` in the same call raises `ValueError` — they populate different lookup tables and picking one keeps the mental model clean.

---

## Messages reference

All messages carry `sender: Process | None`. Pattern-match on the concrete type in callbacks.

```python
@dataclass
class Message:                          # abstract base
    sender: Process | None

@dataclass
class Info(Message):                    # generic async message
    message: Any

@dataclass
class Stop(Message):                    # graceful shutdown request; raises Shutdown internally
    reason: Any

@dataclass
class Exit(Message):                    # delivered when a linked process exits (trap_exits=True)
    reason: Any

@dataclass
class Down(Message):                    # delivered to monitors when the target terminates
    reason: Any

@dataclass
class Call(Message):                    # synchronous request, awaits a reply
    message: Any
    # set_result(value) / await result(timeout) — you don't usually touch these

@dataclass
class Cast(Message):                    # fire-and-forget request
    message: Any

class Ignore:                           # sentinel returned from init to skip the loop
    ...

@dataclass
class Continue:                         # sentinel for handle_continue scheduling
    term: Any
```

`handle_info` receives whatever you sent via `proc.send(...)` or `proc.info(...)` — usually an `Info(sender, payload)` if you used `info()`, or the raw object if you used `send()`. `Call` / `Cast` / `Stop` / `Exit` are intercepted before `handle_info` in `GenServer`.

---

## Exceptions

```python
class Crashed(Exception):
    reason: Any
    def __str__(self) -> str: ...       # str(self.reason)

class Failed(Exception):
    reason: Any                         # supervisor failures: already_started, Max restart intensity, etc.
    def __str__(self) -> str: ...

class Shutdown(Exception):
    reason: Any                         # normal shutdown sentinel; wraps around reason
    def __str__(self) -> str: ...

class AlreadyRegistered(Exception):
    pid: Process                        # raised by Registry.register in unique mode
```

`Crashed` wraps exceptions raised in `init` (preserving the original via `__cause__`); `Runtime.spawn` unwraps `__cause__` before re-raising so callers see the real error type. `_is_normal_shutdown_reason(reason)` is the single source of truth for what counts as a normal exit (matches `"normal"`, `"shutdown"`, or `Shutdown(...)`).

---

## Settings

Runtime defaults live in `fastactor.settings.Settings` (pydantic-settings). Override with `FASTACTOR_`-prefixed env vars.

| Setting        | Env var                  | Default |
| -------------- | ------------------------ | ------- |
| `mailbox_size` | `FASTACTOR_MAILBOX_SIZE` | `1024`  |

```bash
FASTACTOR_MAILBOX_SIZE=4096 uv run python my_app.py
```

The mailbox size caps each process's inbox. `send_nowait` will raise `anyio.WouldBlock` if the mailbox is full.

---

## Idiomatic patterns

### 1. Runtime per program, not per feature

```python
async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        # build your supervision tree here
        ...

if __name__ == "__main__":
    anyio.run(main)
```

Never open two Runtimes concurrently. For tests, use an `async` fixture that enters/exits a single Runtime for the whole test.

### 2. Build a supervision tree top-down

```python
async with Runtime() as rt:
    app_sup = await Supervisor.start_link()
    db = await app_sup.start_child(app_sup.child_spec("db", DbConn, restart="permanent"))
    pool = await app_sup.start_child(app_sup.child_spec(
        "pool",
        DynamicSupervisor,
        kwargs={"max_children": 50},
        restart="permanent",
        type="supervisor",
    ))
```

Hang your supervisor under the runtime root, then hang workers under your supervisor — not directly under the runtime root.

### 3. Deterministic cast sync via call round-trip

```python
# Server must handle "sync" with a non-None reply — e.g., return self.count or True.
# The default base-class `handle_call` returns None, which is NoReply and will hang.
server.cast(("set", key, value))
await server.call("sync")           # round-trip guarantees the cast has been processed
```

The mailbox is strictly FIFO. A successful `call` reply proves every prior `cast` has already been handled. **Use this in tests instead of `asyncio.sleep`.**

### 4. `start_link` by default, `start` for detached work

`start_link` cascades failures through the supervision tree — that's the point of OTP. `start` is for work where a crash must not propagate (audit logging, background cleanup, best-effort emit).

### 5. Trap exits to build a monitor

```python
class Watchdog(GenServer):
    trap_exits = True                   # class-level default or pass at start()
    async def handle_exit(self, msg: Exit):
        logger.error("child %s died: %s", msg.sender, msg.reason)
        # decide: restart? page? escalate?
```

### 6. Task for bounded parallelism

```python
tasks = [await Task.start(fetch, url) for url in urls]
results = [await t for t in tasks]
```

For unbounded parallelism with backpressure, use a `TaskSupervisor` with `max_children`.

### 7. Agent for "just hold this value"

Don't write a full GenServer for a counter. Use `Agent`. If your state manipulation is complex enough to need match/case, promote to GenServer.

---

## Common gotchas

### `handle_call` / `handle_cast` receive wrappers, not raw requests

The callback signature is `handle_call(self, call: Call)`, not `handle_call(self, request, ...)`. Unpack with `call.message`:

```python
# correct
async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
    match call.message:
        case ("get", key): return self.store[key]

# WRONG
async def handle_call(self, request, from_, state): ...
```

### `cast` is synchronous (not awaitable)

`cast` calls `send_nowait` under the hood. It does *not* return a coroutine. Don't `await` it.

```python
server.cast("msg")          # correct
await server.cast("msg")    # TypeError — cast returns None
```

### Only one Runtime at a time

Nesting `async with Runtime():` raises. If you need child runtimes (tests, etc.), make sure each enters and exits cleanly.

### `handle_call` returning `None` means NoReply, not "reply with None"

Under the new contract, a `handle_call` body that falls through to `None` (including the default base-class implementation) does NOT resolve the caller's `await server.call(...)` — the caller stays blocked until something calls `call.set_result(value)`. To reply with the *literal* value `None`, either use a tuple form (`return (None, Continue("ok"))`) or resolve the Call explicitly (`call.set_result(None); return None` — but the second statement is redundant). In practice, pick a meaningful non-None reply (`True`, `"ok"`, the new state).

### `call` timeout raises, doesn't return None

```python
try:
    result = await server.call(req, timeout=1)
except TimeoutError:
    ...
```

### Exceptions in `handle_call` propagate in BOTH directions

The caller sees the exception (good), and the server crashes (also good — this is Elixir semantics). If you want to reply with an error value instead, construct it explicitly:

```python
async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
    try:
        return self._work(call.message)
    except KnownError as e:
        return {"error": str(e)}        # caller gets the dict, server does NOT crash
```

### `_current_process` ContextVar powers the default `sender`

Inside a callback, `_current_process` is set to the handling process. `server.call(req)` from inside `handle_info` defaults `sender` to `self`. You almost never override this manually — but know it's there when debugging messages that appear to come from `None`.

### Normal exit reasons are a whitelist

Only `"normal"`, `"shutdown"`, and `Shutdown(...)` are normal. Anything else — including `None`, `""`, `"ok"` — is treated as abnormal and cascades across links / triggers `transient` restarts.

### `brutal_kill` skips `terminate`

A child with `shutdown="brutal_kill"` has its mailbox closed immediately. `terminate()` won't run to completion, which means monitors may still see `Down` but cleanup you wrote in `terminate` won't fire. Use integer timeouts by default.

### `DynamicSupervisor` only supports `one_for_one`

Passing any other strategy raises `ValueError` in `init`. The error surfaces as the original exception from `start()` — good — but don't waste time trying `"one_for_all"` there.

### `register_name` vs `Registry.register`

Different things:

- `Runtime.register_name` / `Process.start(name=...)` — **one** global name per process. Collides across the whole runtime. Backed by `runtime.registry` / `runtime._reverse_registry`.
- `Registry.register(name, key, proc)` — scoped to a named registry, can have many keys per process, and supports `unique` or `duplicate` modes. Backed by `runtime.registries`.

Use `name=` for singletons. Use `Registry` for everything else.

### `Task.start()` is unlinked — the caller survives a crash

If your task crashes and you don't `await` it, the exception is silently lost. Always `await` a task you care about, or use `Task.start_link(...)` so the cascade kills the caller too.

---

## Testing patterns

See `src/tests/support.py` and `src/tests/conftest.py` for the canonical shapes. Rules of thumb:

- **`pytestmark = pytest.mark.anyio`** at the top of each test file (async tests).
- Autouse an `anyio_backend` fixture returning `"asyncio"`. The repo ships one in `conftest.py`.
- **Scope a single `Runtime`** per test via a fixture:
  ```python
  @pytest.fixture
  async def runtime():
      async with Runtime() as rt:
          yield rt
  ```
- **No `asyncio.sleep` to wait for actor state.** Replace with:
  - `await proc.stopped()` + inspect `_crash_exc` for crash assertions.
  - `await server.call(None)` after casts to force-drain the mailbox (FIFO sync).
  - An `await_child_restart(sup, cid, old_proc)` helper polling on an `anyio.Event` + identity compare for restart tests.
  - A `MonitorServer.await_events(n)` pattern: `handle_info` sets an `anyio.Event` when `len(events) >= n`.
- The full non-e2e suite (114 tests) runs in under a second. There is exactly one intentional `sleep(...)` call — in `test_max_restarts_window_resets_after_max_seconds`, which genuinely needs a time gap — and it's annotated inline.

### Example — crash assertion

```python
proc = await Worker.start()
await proc.send("boom")              # hypothetical: triggers a crash in handle_info
await proc.stopped()
assert isinstance(proc._crash_exc, Crashed)
assert "boom" in str(proc._crash_exc)
```

### Example — restart assertion

```python
old = await sup.start_child(spec)
old.cast(("crash", RuntimeError("die")))
await old.stopped()
# wait for restart — the new process has a different id but the same child_id
await await_child_restart(sup, "worker", old_proc=old)
new = sup.children["worker"].process
assert new is not old
```

---

## Complete public surface

```python
# Top-level (from fastactor import ...)
fastactor.run, fastactor.settings

# from fastactor.otp import ...
# Primitives
Process, GenServer, Agent, Task, TaskSupervisor

# Supervisors
Supervisor, DynamicSupervisor, ChildSpec, RunningChild
RestartStrategy, RestartType, ShutdownType

# Runtime + lookup
Runtime, Registry, whereis

# Messages
Call, Cast, Info, Stop, Exit, Down, Continue

# Sentinels / exceptions
Ignore, Shutdown, Crashed, Failed, AlreadyRegistered
```

---

## Prior art & further reading

Erlang/OTP and Elixir's `GenServer` / `Supervisor` / `DynamicSupervisor` / `Registry` / `Agent` / `Task` modules are the direct inspiration. If you want the "why" behind these primitives:

- ["The Soul of Erlang and Elixir"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvBT4XBdoUE) — short, brilliant talk.
- *Designing for Scalability with Erlang/OTP* (Cesarini & Vinoski, O'Reilly) — the long form.
- Elixir stdlib source under `elixir/lib/elixir/` is the canonical reference for callback semantics.

---

## Source layout (for code diving)

```
src/fastactor/
├── __init__.py
├── settings.py                         # pydantic-settings
├── utils.py                            # id_generator (ksuids)
└── otp/
    ├── __init__.py                     # public re-exports
    ├── _exceptions.py                  # Crashed, Failed, Shutdown, _is_normal_shutdown_reason
    ├── _messages.py                    # Call, Cast, Info, Stop, Exit, Down, Continue, Ignore
    ├── process.py                      # Process (base actor, lifecycle, links/monitors)
    ├── gen_server.py                   # GenServer
    ├── supervisor.py                   # Supervisor, ChildSpec, strategies, restart intensity
    ├── dynamic_supervisor.py           # DynamicSupervisor
    ├── registry.py                     # Registry, AlreadyRegistered
    ├── agent.py                        # Agent
    ├── task.py                         # Task, TaskSupervisor
    └── runtime.py                      # Runtime, RuntimeSupervisor, _current_process ContextVar
```

Reading top-to-bottom: `_messages.py` → `_exceptions.py` → `process.py` → `gen_server.py` → `supervisor.py` → `runtime.py` gives you the full picture. The rest (DynamicSupervisor, Registry, Agent, Task) are additive on top of that core.

---
